text: Our results demonstrated the sustainable and economical green synthesis of zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO-NPs) using bioactive polyphenols present in *Phoenix dactylifera L.* (date palm) leaf extract as eco-friendly reducing and stabilizing agents. Different compositions in relation to the concentration of zinc source and plant extract were studied to obtain an optimal synthesis method of the NP with the desired properties. In addition, the synthesized NP samples were evaluated by XRD, TEM, and SEM analyses to determine crystal structure, shape, size distribution, and morphology. The particle size ranges of the ZnO-NPs were found to be between 18.1 and 61.6 nm, depending on the amounts of zinc source and the date palm leaf extract used. Based on the TEM results, the obtained samples exhibited different shapes, including predominantly spherical and cubic and hexagonal nanorods. FTIR spectra obtained from the NPs exhibited a range of characteristic peaks of several plant-based polyphenols that may suggest their involvement in the reducing as well as capping of NP surfaces. Biological evaluations indicated the effectiveness of the green-synthesized ZnO-NPs as a radical scavenger for DPPH assay with high total antioxidant capacity (TAC). The NPs exhibited significant antibacterial activities against both Gram-positive *Staphylococcus aureus* and Gram-negative *Escherichia coli* bacteria. Therefore, our studies suggested that plant-based green synthesis routes such as the use of date palm extracts could be adopted as eco-friendly and biocompatible alternatives in the place of the commonly used hazardous synthetic chemicals for producing effective biomedical NPs for pharmaceutical applications.
